description: With distal 'ra', the speaker points beyond the immediate frame: that person, that place, that time, or an earlier matter in the discourse. It selects the farther referent before a noun. Used alone with 'nai' (be), it supplies there as a location predicate; 'ha' (PROX) is the near counterpart.
articulatory_notes: The tongue tip taps or briefly trills at the alveolar ridge for 'r', then lowers into unrounded open-central 'a'. Full stress rests on this single syllable.
sound_symbolism: The tap or trill briefly reaches forward before the open vowel. A speaker may hear a little distance in that motion.
usage_notes: Wider Slot 2 particles 'we' (ALSO) and 'li' (RESTR) stand before 'ra'; plurality, numerals, focus, and degree come after it. Number leaves the form unchanged: 'ra melu' means that friend, while 'ra lo melu' means those friends. A locational phrase supplies any finer distance than Phi's proximal and distal contrast.
